message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The SARS-CoV-2 Main protease (M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  ) is of major interest as an anti-viral drug target. Structure-based virtual screening efforts, fueled by a growing list of apo and inhibitor-bound SARS-CoV/CoV-2 M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  crystal structures, are underway in many labs. However, little is known about the dynamic enzyme mechanism, which is needed to inform both structure-based design and assay development. Here, we apply Biodynamics theory to characterize the structural dynamics of substrate-induced M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  activation, and explore the implications thereof for efficacious inhibition under non-equilibrium conditions. The catalytic cycle (including tetrahedral intermediate formation and hydrolysis) is governed by
                  <jats:underline>concerted</jats:underline>
                  dynamic structural rearrangements of domain 3 and the m-shaped loop (residues 132-147) on which Cys145 (comprising the thiolate nucleophile and one-half of the oxyanion hole) and Gly143 reside (comprising the other half of the oxyanion hole). In particular:
                  <jats:list list-type="order">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        Domain 3 undergoes dynamic rigid-body rotations about the domain 2-3 linker, alternately visiting two conformational states (denoted as
                        <jats:inline-formula>
                          <jats:alternatives>
                            <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="211185v1_inline1.gif"/>
                          </jats:alternatives>
                        </jats:inline-formula>
                        ).
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        The Gly143-containing crest of the m-shaped loop (denoted as crest B) undergoes up and down translations in concert with the domain 3 rotations (denoted as
                        <jats:inline-formula>
                          <jats:alternatives>
                            <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="211185v1_inline2.gif"/>
                          </jats:alternatives>
                        </jats:inline-formula>
                        , whereas the Cys145-containing crest (denoted as crest A) remains statically in the up position. The crest B translations are driven by conformational transitions within the rising leg of the loop (Lys137-Asn142).
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  We propose that substrates associate to the
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="211185v1_inline3.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  state, which promotes the
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="211185v1_inline4.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  state, dimerization (denoted as
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="211185v1_inline5.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  -substrate), and catalysis. The structure resets to the dynamic monomeric form upon dissociation of the N-terminal product. We describe the energetics of the aforementioned state transitions, and address the implications of our proposed mechanism for efficacious M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  inhibition under native-like conditions.
                </jats:p>
title: Probing the dynamic structure-function and structure-free energy relationships of the corona virus main protease with Biodynamics theory

reference.article-title: THE MODE OF ACTION OF UREASE AND OF ENZYMES IN GENERAL
On the Mode of Activation of the Catalytically Essential Sulfhydryl Group of Papain
